target: PRED-090 imminent test window assessment: NH TOA flux excess >0.3 W/m² in April 2026

assessment.prediction_claim: Northern hemisphere TOA radiative flux exceeds SH by >0.3 W/m² in April 2026
assessment.scientific_context: The hemispheric asymmetry in TOA (top-of-atmosphere) net radiative flux is a well-documented feature of Earth's energy budget. Loeb et al. (2016, Nature Geoscience) showed NH absorbs ~0.2 W/m² more than SH annually, with seasonal amplitudes of ±5 W/m². In April (NH spring), the NH receives increasingly more insolation as summer approaches. CERES EBAF data (Edition 4.2) consistently shows NH net flux exceeding SH in April by 2-4 W/m² due to seasonal solar geometry plus the albedo asymmetry from NH land mass vs SH ocean. The dome's 0.3 W/m² threshold is trivially easy to meet — the actual asymmetry is an order of magnitude larger.
assessment.kernel_of_truth: The hemispheric energy asymmetry IS real and scientifically interesting. Stephens et al. (2015) documented that Earth maintains remarkable hemispheric energy balance (within ~0.2 W/m² annually) despite dramatically different surface compositions. The dome model correctly identifies this asymmetry as a real phenomenon. However, standard climate science has studied this exhaustively since the CERES instrument suite launched (2000). The dome model provides no mechanism — it just adopts the observation.
assessment.likely_outcome: WILL PASS — but trivially. NH TOA flux exceeds SH by 2-4 W/m² every April. The 0.3 W/m² threshold is set so low that failure is essentially impossible. This is a textbook example of a "prediction" that cannot fail because the threshold is well below the known signal. CERES monthly data for April 2026 should be available ~June 2026 (2-month processing lag).
assessment.data_availability: CERES EBAF Ed4.2 monthly data has ~2-month latency. April 2026 data expected ~June 2026. Near-real-time CERES SSF1deg data may be available sooner but with lower accuracy. NASA Langley CERES data portal: ceres.larc.nasa.gov.
assessment.recommendation: Do NOT wait for April data to close this. The prediction is post-hoc (hemispheric asymmetry is known for decades) with an absurdly low threshold. Update predictions.json entry with assessment when CERES data arrives (~June 2026). Flag for poller to check CERES data portal. The dome will almost certainly score this as "confirmed" — our response should be pre-written: standard climate science has predicted this since Loeb et al. (2016), the threshold is set to guarantee success, and WIN-066 already covers the same claim.
assessment.standard_physics_prediction: NH TOA net flux exceeds SH by 2-4 W/m² in April, driven by seasonal solar geometry (NH spring insolation increase), land-ocean albedo contrast, and cloud distribution. This has been observed consistently in every April since CERES became operational (2000). The prediction threshold of 0.3 W/m² is approximately 10× below the typical observed signal.

verdict_preview: When data arrives: Std Model Explains. The hemispheric TOA flux asymmetry is a cornerstone finding of CERES-era climate science, not a dome prediction.
